line.token: // GET /products/by-asin/{asin}/{region} - Get product by ASIN and region
// GET /products/by-asin/{asin}/{region}/prices - Get price history by ASIN/region
* Get collection params for products list
// regardless of the source column's collation, so a plain LIKE here
// would silently miss any case-differing match (e.g. searching
// "wireless" would never find a product titled "Wireless Mouse").
// LOWER() on both sides sidesteps the collation mismatch entirely,
// rather than hardcoding a COLLATE that may not match every
"(LOWER(JSON_UNQUOTE(JSON_EXTRACT(p.facts, '$.title'))) LIKE LOWER(%s) OR LOWER(JSON_UNQUOTE(JSON_EXTRACT(p.facts, '$.brand'))) LIKE LOWER(%s))"
// Map sort field to actual column
// Get products with latest price using optimized correlated subquery
// This pattern leverages the product_recorded (product_id, recorded_at) composite index
// for efficient "latest row per group" lookups
// Check rate limit for non-admin users
// Check if already exists (active or previously soft-deleted)
// Lets the caller confirm the ASIN hasn't been recycled for a
// different product before deciding whether to reactivate it.
// Use Product Service to create the product (fetches from Amazon Creators API)
// Add rate limit headers for non-admin users
* Distinct from create_item() (rather than having create silently revive a
* soft-deleted row) so reactivation is separately observable/auditable,
* and so a caller who hits the 409 from create_item() makes an explicit,
* deliberate choice to bring the existing product back rather than it
* happening as a side effect of a create call.
* Re-fetches from Amazon (same as create) rather than just flipping
* is_active, since the previously stored data may be stale. Subject to
* the same blacklist check as create_item(). Also subject to the same
* rate limit *gate* for non-admins (blocked once already at today's cap),
* though note it does not itself count towards that cap the way create
* does - it updates the existing row rather than inserting a new one, so
* there's no new created_at to count.
// Detect and remove duplicates within the request to avoid wasted API calls
'Duplicate ASIN/region combination in the same request'
// Create a mock request for each product
// Add rate limit headers for non-admin users
// Hard delete - remove product and all price history
// Soft delete - set is_active to false
* Get product by ASIN and region
* Get product by ASIN and region
* Check rate limit for current user
* Get rate limit info for current user
* Check if user has partner tag for region
* Get price history for a product
// $date_format is spliced directly into $sql below, which then goes
// through $db->prepare(). wpdb::prepare() scans the whole query
// string for its own %s/%d/etc. placeholders, and '%d' in '%Y-%m-%d'
// (the 'daily' and default cases) is indistinguishable from a real
// integer placeholder to that scan - it throws off the
// placeholder/argument count and prepare() returns null. Doubling
// the percent signs here is wpdb's documented escape for a literal
// '%' so DATE_FORMAT() still receives the real format string.
* Format product summary for list response
